headlines.geopolitics: Navy veteran with no family gets full military honors as community refuses to let him be buried alone
headlines.economy: DOJ launches new fraud division targeting 'insane' Medicaid and federal program theft
headlines.domestic: Americans expect AI to transform everything but find it mostly useless in daily life

metadata.daily_do: Check if your local VA cemetery has upcoming services for unclaimed veterans — showing up matters more than you think

clips.caption: When Duke gets unexpectedly emotional about veterans
Pringle explains why we honor unknown soldiers
Jax discovers government AI is being used for office chairs

script.text: Look, I've been to a lot of military funerals. But when three hundred strangers show up for a guy they never met? That's not performative anything. That's just America.
It's Monday, March tenth. I'm Leo, and this is The Friction. Three stories driving the conversation today.

First up — a Navy veteran in Tennessee died with no known family to claim his remains. The community wasn't having it. Hundreds showed up to give Lonnie Wayman full military honors at Middle Tennessee State Veterans Cemetery.
Oh wow.
Good.
The VA rep said quote, 'unclaimed does not mean forgotten.' They released doves at the end.
I'm not crying, you're crying.

Economy — Deputy AG Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division to tackle what he calls an 'insane' problem. Two guys from Pennsylvania drove to Minneapolis, pretended to run homeless shelters that didn't exist, and walked away with four million in federal funds.
Four million? Just like that?
That's horrifying.
Blanche says they're going after any fraud over a thousand dollars now. Quote, 'if you steal one thousand and one dollars, you are going to prison.'
Very specific number there, Todd.

And domestic — a new Fox poll shows eighty-seven percent of Americans think AI will transform our lives, but only forty-seven percent find it useful right now. Meanwhile, eighty percent are extremely or very worried about telling real content from AI-generated stuff.
I mean, fair.
The promise versus the reality. Tale as old as time.
Also, seventy-four percent of voters think life exists on other planets. So there's that.
Honestly shocked it's not higher. Have you seen Congress lately?

Let's dig into that Tennessee story because it's hitting me harder than I expected. A Navy vet dies alone, no family comes forward, and instead of a quiet burial, three hundred strangers show up. Duke, you served — what's your read on this?
Look, I've been to a lot of military funerals. But when three hundred strangers show up for a guy they never met? That's not performative anything. That's just America.
I want to believe that. But I also saw this story go viral on social media first. Like, how many of those people would've shown up without the Facebook event?
Does it matter? The man got honors. He got dignity. He didn't get buried alone.
It matters if we're patting ourselves on the back for fixing a problem we created. How does a veteran die completely alone in the first place?
That's the question, isn't it? What happened in this man's life that he had no connections when he died?

Some people just— life happens, you know? Not everyone has kids. Not everyone stays in touch with their unit. Jenny's dad served in Vietnam, came home, never talked about it. Barely talked to anyone for decades.
Right, and that's exactly what I'm talking about. We send people to war, they come back changed, we don't support them properly, and then when they die alone we show up with flags and call it honor.
Bree, come on. You think those people at the cemetery were thinking about politics? They were thinking about a fellow American who served his country.
I'm not saying their hearts weren't in the right place. I'm saying maybe our hearts should be in the right place while these guys are still alive.
Can I just— this is gonna sound weird, but I keep thinking about the doves. Like, who decided on the doves? Was there a committee meeting about funeral doves?
Jax, not now.
No, I'm serious! Someone made that decision. Someone cared enough to think about the details. That matters.
That's exactly right. Someone thought about making it meaningful.

Look, I'm not trying to be cynical here. The funeral was beautiful. I just think we can hold two thoughts at once — celebrate the community response and ask why we need community responses in the first place.
The VA says there are about a thousand unclaimed veterans nationwide at any given time. So this isn't a one-off situation.
A thousand. Jesus.
See? That's a systemic issue. That's not individual failure, that's institutional failure.
Maybe. But you know what's not institutional? Those three hundred people who showed up. That's individual choice. That's character.
The question becomes — how do we scale that character? How do we make sure it happens for all thousand of those veterans, not just the ones that go viral?
Better mental health support. Better community outreach while they're alive. Better—
Better personal responsibility too. I'm sorry, but some of this is on us as individuals. Check on your neighbors. Know who lives on your street.
Duke just solved loneliness with suburban nosiness. I respect that.
Mock me all you want. When's the last time you talked to your neighbors?
Um. My neighbor's a cat named The Situation, so, this morning.
I actually think Duke's right about that part. My building has a group chat now. It's mostly noise complaints, but still.
Alright, so the Tennessee story gives us both — institutional failure and individual response. The question is whether three hundred people showing up makes the failure better or just more visible.
I'll take visible failure over invisible failure any day. At least visible failure gets addressed.
Professor, this has to connect to something in history — communities honoring unknown or unclaimed service members.
Indeed it does, and quite directly. The Tomb of the Unknown Soldier at Arlington — dedicated in nineteen twenty-one — emerged from precisely this impulse.
After the Great War, we had thousands of unidentified remains. Families back home had no grave to visit, no place to mourn. The Army realized this was, in Duke's parlance, a systemic problem.
Huh. So they made one grave represent all of them?
Precisely. One unknown soldier, representing all unknown soldiers. But here's the fascinating part — they didn't just bury him quietly. They made it the most honored grave in America.
Twenty-four hour guard. Changing of the guard ceremony. Wreath laying by presidents. The most anonymous soldier in American history became the most famous.
That's actually beautiful. And it serves the living families, not just the dead soldier.
Exactly right, Miss Aldana. It gave every Gold Star family a place to mourn their unidentified son. It transformed individual grief into collective mourning.
Wait, so we've been doing the community funeral thing for over a hundred years?

In a sense, yes. Though the tomb honors the unknown, not the unclaimed. There's a distinction. Unknown soldiers died in service. Unclaimed veterans often died in peacetime, forgotten by circumstance rather than war.
So the Tennessee funeral is actually filling a gap that the tomb tradition doesn't cover.
Quite so. The tomb says 'we honor our war dead.' The Tennessee funeral says 'we honor our veterans, period.' It's an evolution of the same principle.
That makes me feel better about it, actually. Like there's precedent for caring about people you never met.

There's more. In ancient Rome, soldiers who died far from home were buried with coins over their eyes — payment for Charon to ferry them across the river Styx. The community took responsibility for the dead soldier's journey.

Every culture has some version of this. The Scots have the 'Unknown Warrior' at Edinburgh Castle. The French have their 'Soldat Inconnu' under the Arc de Triomphe. It's a human constant — the community claims the unclaimed.
So maybe what happened in Tennessee wasn't performative. Maybe it was just... human nature?
I would argue it's both. Performance in the best sense — a ritual that serves a social function. Theater that creates meaning.
Doc just made me feel smarter about crying over a news story. Thank you.
The tears are the point, young man. Grief shared is grief diminished. The Romans knew this. So did the people of Tennessee.

Alright, time to cleanse the palate with some truly absurd news. First up — a boss in Ireland repeatedly shouted the word 'potato' at his Irish employee and somehow thought this wouldn't be a harassment lawsuit.
Just... potato? Like, as an insult?
Apparently! The employment tribunal said it created a 'hostile work environment.' I mean, yeah. If my boss kept yelling vegetables at me, I'd be hostile too.
That's just straight-up ethnic harassment. Not funny at all.
You're absolutely right. Moving on — remember Palantir's scary AI weapons system that everyone was worried about? Well, it's now being used to help government workers find office chairs.
I'm sorry, what?

I kid you not. The same AI that was designed to identify enemy combatants is now helping the General Services Administration optimize furniture procurement. From targeting humans to targeting ergonomic seating solutions.
That's the most government thing I've ever heard. Take a two-billion-dollar weapons system and use it for office supplies.
Honestly? I prefer this. Less murder, more lumbar support.
Swords into plowshares. Or in this case, algorithms into adjustable desk chairs.

Next — Louisiana schools have until Friday to post the Ten Commandments in every classroom, and apparently nobody thought about what happens if you're Jewish or Muslim or, you know, care about the First Amendment.
That's heading straight to the Supreme Court.
I mean, look, I'm a religious guy, but you can't just mandate one religion in public schools. That's not how this works.
Thank you. Also, which version of the Ten Commandments? Catholic? Protestant? Jewish? They're not all the same.
Oh, and final story — a Florida drug dealer locked his customer in a storage unit for months over an unpaid debt, which I guess is one way to handle collections.
Jesus Christ.
The guy survived on what the dealer brought him, which apparently was not a lot. He's in the hospital now, the dealer's in jail, and I have so many questions about the logistics of this operation.
Like, did he pay rent on the storage unit? Was that factored into the debt?
Duke, no. This is kidnapping and torture, not a business model.
You're right. That was dark, even for me.
Florida continues to be the state where the laws of physics and human decency go to die.
Alright, your Daily Do. That Tennessee story got me thinking — there are probably unclaimed veteran services happening near you that could use community support.
Check your local VA cemetery website or call them directly. Many host services for unclaimed veterans throughout the year, and they're always looking for community members to attend.
You don't have to be a veteran. You don't have to know the person. You just have to show up. Sometimes that's enough.
That's a good one, Leo. I'm gonna look into that myself.
As Marcus Aurelius wrote, 'What brings no benefit to the hive can bring none to the bee.' Sometimes being part of the hive means showing up for someone you never met.
That's The Friction. We're back tomorrow with more news that matters and jokes that probably don't. I'm Jax, and remember — if you steal one thousand and one dollars, Todd Blanche is coming for you.
Very specific threat there, Jax.
Hey, I don't make the rules. I just mock them. See you tomorrow.
